content.text: What a Resource Group Is

A resource group is a logical container for Azure resources within a subscription. Every Azure resource — virtual machines, databases, virtual networks, storage accounts, web apps — must belong to exactly one resource group. The resource group provides three capabilities: a lifecycle boundary (deleting a group deletes all resources in it), a management scope (RBAC roles and Azure Policy can be assigned at the resource group level), and an organizational unit for billing and operational filtering.

A resource group has a location (Azure region), but this location stores only the resource group metadata — the record of which resources belong to it. The resources within the group can be in any Azure region. A resource group named 'myapp-prod' with its location set to East US can contain a VM in East US, a storage account in West US, and a key vault in North Europe.

attrs.alt: Resource groups as logical containers within a subscription: web-rg (VMs, LB, NSG), data-rg (SQL, Storage, KeyVault), ops-rg (shared monitoring resources)

content.text: Lifecycle Management: The Primary Purpose of Resource Groups

The most important property of a resource group is that deleting it deletes all resources inside it atomically. This makes resource groups the correct unit of lifecycle management: when a project ends, when a test environment is torn down, or when an application is decommissioned, deleting the resource group removes all associated resources in a single operation without having to enumerate and delete each resource individually.

The delete operation is parallel and atomic within the ARM (Azure Resource Manager) layer: ARM sends delete operations to all resources in the group simultaneously and waits for all deletions to complete before reporting success. If any resource fails to delete (e.g., a resource with a delete lock), the operation fails and the resource group is left in a partially deleted state that requires manual cleanup.

# Create a resource group
az group create \
  --name myapp-prod-web \
  --location eastus \
  --tags Environment=Production App=myapp Tier=web Owner=platform-team

# List all resource groups in the subscription
az group list \
  --query "[].{name:name, location:location, state:properties.provisioningState}" \
  --output table

# List all resources inside a specific resource group
az resource list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --query "[].{name:name, type:type, location:location}" \
  --output table

# Delete a resource group and ALL resources in it (irreversible)
# The --yes flag skips the confirmation prompt (use carefully in scripts)
az group delete --name myapp-prod-web --yes --no-wait

# Check deletion progress
az group show --name myapp-prod-web --query properties.provisioningState -o tsv
# Returns "Deleting" while in progress, then errors with ResourceGroupNotFound when complete

az group delete is irreversible. There is no undo, no recycle bin, and no recovery period (unlike subscription cancellation). Always ensure the resource group name is correct before running the delete command, and verify that no critical resources were inadvertently included.
Resource Group Design Patterns
How you partition resources across resource groups determines how easily you can manage, monitor, and delete them. Three common patterns:
Pattern 1: Group by application and environment

Each application has its own resource groups, one per environment: myapp-prod, myapp-staging, myapp-dev. Resources belonging to the same deployment of the application live in the same group, enabling single-command teardown of each environment.
Pattern 2: Group by tier

Resources are grouped by technical tier: web-rg (VMs, load balancers, NSGs), data-rg (SQL databases, storage accounts), ops-rg (monitoring, automation). This pattern makes sense when the lifecycle of tiers differs — the web tier is redeployed frequently, the data tier persists across web deployments.
Pattern 3: Hybrid (application + tier)

Combine both: myapp-prod-web, myapp-prod-data, myapp-prod-ops. This gives per-tier lifecycle control while keeping environments isolated. It results in more resource groups to manage, but the clarity usually justifies the overhead.

# Create a set of resource groups following the hybrid pattern
APP="myapp"
ENV="prod"
LOCATION="eastus"

for TIER in web data ops; do
  az group create \
    --name "${APP}-${ENV}-${TIER}" \
    --location "$LOCATION" \
    --tags App="$APP" Environment="$ENV" Tier="$TIER" Owner="platform-team"
done

# Deploy resources to the appropriate group
# Web tier: VM + Load Balancer
az vm create \
  --resource-group "${APP}-${ENV}-web" \
  --name "${APP}-web-01" \
  --location "$LOCATION" \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D2s_v3 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys \
  --no-wait

# Data tier: Azure SQL
az sql server create \
  --resource-group "${APP}-${ENV}-data" \
  --name "${APP}-sqlserver" \
  --location "$LOCATION" \
  --admin-user sqladmin \
  --admin-password "$(openssl rand -base64 16)!"

# Verify all groups created
az group list \
  --query "[?starts_with(name, '${APP}-${ENV}')].{name:name, location:location}" \
  --output table
Resource Locks
Azure Resource Manager supports two types of locks that can be applied at the resource group or individual resource level:
ReadOnly lock:

prevents any modification or deletion of the resource or resource group. All PUT, POST, PATCH, and DELETE operations are denied. Read (GET) operations succeed. This is appropriate for production resource groups where no configuration changes should happen without an explicit lock removal step.
CanNotDelete lock:

allows reads and modifications, but prevents deletion. This protects against accidental az group delete while still allowing configuration changes. Appropriate for production databases, key vaults, and other stateful resources that must not be deleted accidentally.

# Add a CanNotDelete lock to a production resource group
az lock create \
  --name "prod-delete-lock" \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-data \
  --notes "Prevents accidental deletion of production data tier"

# Add a ReadOnly lock to freeze a resource group configuration
az lock create \
  --name "prod-readonly-lock" \
  --lock-type ReadOnly \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web

# List all locks on a resource group
az lock list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-data \
  --query "[].{name:name, type:properties.level, notes:properties.notes}" \
  --output table

# Remove a lock (required before deleting a locked resource group)
az lock delete \
  --name "prod-delete-lock" \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-data

# Locks are inherited: a lock on a resource group applies to all resources in it
# List all locks including inherited
az lock list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --query "[].{name:name, level:properties.level, scope:id}" \
  --output table

Resource locks override RBAC. Even an Owner cannot delete a CanNotDelete-locked resource without first removing the lock. The Owner can remove the lock and then delete, but this extra step prevents accidental deletions in automated scripts that would fail on a locked resource.
Moving Resources Between Resource Groups

Resources can be moved between resource groups (within the same subscription or to a different subscription) using az resource move. Not all resource types support move operations, and some moves have restrictions (e.g., Azure SQL servers can be moved but their databases move with the server; you cannot move individual databases independently).

# Move a VM and its NIC from one resource group to another
# First, get the resource IDs to move
VM_ID=$(az vm show --resource-group myapp-old-rg --name myvm --query id -o tsv)
NIC_ID=$(az vm show --resource-group myapp-old-rg --name myvm --query "networkProfile.networkInterfaces[0].id" -o tsv)

# Validate the move before executing (dry run check)
az resource invoke-action \
  --action validateMoveResources \
  --ids "$VM_ID" \
  --request-body "{
    "targetResourceGroup": "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-new-rg",
    "resources": ["$VM_ID", "$NIC_ID"]
  }"

# Execute the move
az resource move \
  --destination-group myapp-new-rg \
  --ids "$VM_ID" "$NIC_ID"

# Verify resources appear in new group
az resource list \
  --resource-group myapp-new-rg \
  --query "[?name=='myvm'].{name:name, type:type, rg:resourceGroup}" \
  --output table
Tagging Resources and Resource Groups

Tags are key-value pairs applied to Azure resources and resource groups for cost allocation, operational queries, and governance enforcement. Tags on a resource group are NOT automatically inherited by resources inside it — you must apply tags to each resource individually, or use Azure Policy to enforce consistent tagging.

# Apply tags to a resource group
az group update \
  --name myapp-prod-web \
  --set tags.Owner="web-team@contoso.com" tags.CostCenter="CC-1234" tags.Classification="Internal"

# Apply the same tags to all resources in a group (requires iterating each resource)
TAGS="Owner=web-team@contoso.com CostCenter=CC-1234 Classification=Internal"
az resource list --resource-group myapp-prod-web --query "[].id" -o tsv | while read ID; do
  az resource tag --ids "$ID" --tags $TAGS --is-incremental
done

# Azure Policy: require CostCenter tag on all resources in a subscription
az policy assignment create \
  --name "require-costcenter-tag" \
  --policy "1e30110a-5ceb-460c-a204-c1c3969c6d62" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "CostCenter"}}'

# Find all resources missing a specific tag (for compliance audit)
az resource list \
  --query "[?tags.CostCenter==null].{name:name, type:type, rg:resourceGroup}" \
  --output table
Resource Group Naming Conventions

Azure enforces constraints on resource group names: 1–90 characters, alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores, parentheses, and periods only, and cannot end with a period. Within those limits, a consistent naming convention prevents ambiguity, enables automation, and makes az group list output readable.

A widely adopted pattern: {workload}-{environment}-{tier} or {company}-{region}-{environment}-{workload}. Whatever pattern you choose, document it and enforce it with Azure Policy using the 'Match naming convention' built-in definition.

# Common naming pattern: {app}-{env}-{tier}
# Examples:
#   payments-prod-web
#   payments-prod-data
#   payments-staging-web
#   shared-ops-monitoring

# Enforce naming convention via Azure Policy
# Built-in: 'Resource Group names must match naming convention'
az policy definition list \
  --query "[?displayName=='Require a tag and its value on resources'].{name:name, id:id}" \
  --output table

# Create a custom policy to enforce resource group naming pattern
az policy definition create \
  --name "enforce-rg-naming" \
  --display-name "Enforce resource group naming pattern" \
  --description "Resource groups must follow pattern: {app}-{env}-{tier}" \
  --rules '{
    "if": {
      "allOf": [
        {"field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups"},
        {"not": {"field": "name", "match": "?????-????-???"}}
      ]
    },
    "then": {"effect": "deny"}
  }' \
  --mode All

# List resource groups sorted by name (useful for auditing naming consistency)
az group list --query "sort_by([].{name:name, location:location}, &name)" --output table
Querying and Exporting Resource Group Contents

Understanding what is inside a resource group before deleting or modifying it is essential for safe operations. Azure Resource Graph provides fast, tenant-wide queries against resource group contents across all subscriptions.

# List all resources in a resource group with their types and locations
az resource list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --output table

# Get resource count by type in a resource group
az resource list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --query "group_by([].type, &[0]) | keys(@)" \
  --output tsv

# Azure Resource Graph query: all resource groups with no resources (candidates for cleanup)
az graph query -q "
  ResourceContainers
  | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups'
  | join kind=leftouter (
      Resources | summarize count() by resourceGroup
    ) on resourceGroup
  | where isnull(count_)
  | project name, subscriptionId, location
  | order by name asc
" --output table

# Export a resource group's ARM template (useful for migration or documentation)
az group export \
  --name myapp-prod-web \
  --output json > myapp-prod-web-template.json

# List all resource groups across ALL subscriptions (requires tenant-level access)
az account list --query "[].id" -o tsv | while read SUB_ID; do
  az group list --subscription "$SUB_ID" \
    --query "[].{sub:'$SUB_ID', name:name, location:location}" \
    --output tsv
done
Resource Group and ARM Template Deployment

Azure Resource Manager (ARM) templates and Bicep files are deployed at a scope — the most common scope is the resource group. When you deploy a template at resource group scope, ARM creates or updates all resources defined in the template within that resource group. If a resource defined in the template does not exist, it is created; if it exists, ARM compares the desired state to the current state and updates only what differs (idempotent deployment).

# Deploy a Bicep file to a resource group
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @parameters.json \
  --mode Incremental

# Incremental mode (default): creates/updates resources in template; does not delete existing resources not in template
# Complete mode: creates/updates resources in template AND DELETES resources in the group not in the template
# Use Complete mode carefully — it will delete manually created resources

# Validate a template before deploying (dry run; shows what would change)
az deployment group validate \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @parameters.json

# What-if: show planned changes before deploying
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @parameters.json

# Show deployment history for a resource group
az deployment group list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --query "[].{name:name, state:properties.provisioningState, timestamp:properties.timestamp}" \
  --output table

# Roll back to a previous successful deployment
az deployment group rollback \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --name previous-deployment-name

ARM and Bicep deployments to a resource group are idempotent in Incremental mode — re-running the same template is safe and only applies needed changes. This is the foundation of GitOps for Azure infrastructure: the template in the repository is the source of truth, and CI/CD pipelines apply it on every merge.
Resource Group RBAC in Practice

RBAC at the resource group scope is the most granular scope at which you typically delegate administrative access to a team. Assigning Contributor to a team at the resource group scope lets them manage everything inside it without touching other resource groups in the subscription.
Scenario
Recommended scope
Role
Application team manages their own web tier only
web-rg resource group
Contributor
DBA team manages only SQL databases
data-rg resource group
SQL DB Contributor (built-in)
Security team read-only access across all production
Subscription or management group
Reader
DevOps pipeline deploys to specific resource groups
Each target resource group
Contributor on each group
Operations team can read monitoring but not modify infra
ops-rg resource group
Monitoring Reader
Central networking team manages VNets and NSGs only
Specific resource groups containing network resources
Network Contributor

# Assign Contributor to a team group at the resource group scope
az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee-object-id "$(az ad group show --group 'web-team' --query id -o tsv)" \
  --assignee-principal-type Group \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-prod-web"

# Assign SQL DB Contributor to a DBA group at the data resource group scope
az role assignment create \
  --role "SQL DB Contributor" \
  --assignee-object-id "$(az ad group show --group 'dba-team' --query id -o tsv)" \
  --assignee-principal-type Group \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-prod-data"

# View effective role assignments on a resource group (includes inherited from subscription)
az role assignment list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myapp-prod-web" \
  --include-inherited \
  --query "[].{principal:principalName, role:roleDefinitionName, scope:scope}" \
  --output table

# Remove a stale role assignment
az role assignment delete \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee "former-team-member@contoso.com" \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web
Common Resource Group Anti-Patterns
One resource group for everything:

putting all resources for an entire subscription in a single resource group makes lifecycle management impossible — you cannot delete half the contents. It also prevents per-group RBAC delegation and makes az resource list output unworkable.
Using resource groups as environments within the same subscription:

naming resource groups 'prod' and 'staging' in the same subscription does not create real isolation. A Contributor on the subscription can modify both regardless of resource group names. Use separate subscriptions for true environment isolation.
Forgetting to group dependent resources:

a VM and its managed disk, NIC, and NSG should be in the same resource group. If you put the VM in web-rg and the NIC in networking-rg, deleting web-rg leaves orphaned NICs and creates reference errors for future deployments.
Using resource groups as cost allocation units:

resource groups provide filtering in Azure Cost Management, but tags are the correct mechanism for granular cost allocation. Resource groups are lifecycle units; tags are cost metadata. Design each independently.
Never cleaning up empty resource groups:

after tests, experiments, or decommissioned projects, empty resource groups accumulate. They do not generate cost, but they clutter az group list and make it harder to audit the subscription. Schedule periodic cleanup using Azure Resource Graph to find empty groups.
Resource Group Limits
Azure enforces hard limits on resource groups and their contents:
Limit
Value
Notes
Resource groups per subscription
Hard limit; cannot be increased
Resources per resource group (per type)
Limit is per resource type (provider/type combination), not total resources
Resource locks per resource or resource group
Per scope
Deployments per resource group
Deployment history; oldest are auto-purged after 800
Resource group name length
1–90 characters
Alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores, parentheses, periods; cannot end with period
Tags per resource group
Tag key max 512 chars; value max 256 chars

# Check how many resource groups are in the subscription
az group list --query "length(@)" -o tsv

# Count resources per type in a resource group (check proximity to 800 limit)
az resource list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --query "group_by([].type, &[0]) | to_entries(@)[].{type:key, count:length(value)}" \
  --output table

# Check deployment history count (approaches 800 limit in active groups)
az deployment group list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --query "length(@)" -o tsv

# Clean up old deployment history to stay below the 800 limit
# List deployments older than 90 days
az deployment group list \
  --resource-group myapp-prod-web \
  --query "[?properties.timestamp < '2024-01-01'].name" \
  -o tsv | while read DEPL; do
    az deployment group delete --name "$DEPL" --resource-group myapp-prod-web --no-wait
  done
Summary

Azure Resource Groups are logical containers that group related Azure resources within a subscription. They provide lifecycle management (deleting the group deletes all contents), a scope for RBAC and Policy, and an organizational unit for operational tasks.
Every resource belongs to exactly one resource group; the group's region stores metadata only — resources inside can be in any region.
Deleting a resource group atomically deletes all resources in it — this is the correct way to clean up entire environments or decommissioned applications.
Group resources by lifecycle similarity: things deployed, managed, and deleted together belong in the same group.
Use CanNotDelete locks on production data-tier resource groups; use ReadOnly locks on resource groups whose configuration must be frozen.
Resources can be moved between resource groups, but not all resource types support move; validate first with az resource invoke-action --action validateMoveResources.
Tags on a resource group are not inherited by resources inside it; use Azure Policy to enforce consistent resource-level tagging.
